Pittsburgh Steelers:
{{Short description|none}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} The [[Pittsburgh Steelers]] franchise has had 17 head coaches throughout its history. Founded as the Pittsburgh Pirates in [[1933 Pittsburgh Pirates (NFL) season|1933]],{{cite web|url= http://www.nfl.com/history/chronology/1931-1940#1933|title= NFL history 1933|website= NFL.com|access-date= April 27, 2008|archive-date= May 10, 2020|archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20200510223235/http://www.nfl.com/history/chronology/1931-1940#1933|url-status= live}} the name was changed to the Steelers prior to the [[1940 Pittsburgh Steelers season|1940]] season to celebrate the city's heritage of producing [[Steel mill|steel]].{{cite web|url=http://news.steelers.com/MediaContent/2007/08/22/05/Steelers_History_80311.pdf |title=Steelers' History |access-date=April 27, 2008 |publisher=Pittsburgh Steelers |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080528074040/http://news.steelers.com/MediaContent/2007/08/22/05/Steelers_History_80311.pdf |archive-date=May 28, 2008 }} [[Joe Bach]] served two separate terms as head coach and [[Walt Kiesling]] served three separate terms. During the {{nfly|1943}} and {{nfly|1944}} seasons, due to the number of [[History of American football#War years|players who fought]] in [[World War II]], the Steelers combined their team with [[Steagles|Philadelphia]] and [[Card-Pitt|Chicago]], respectively. During these seasons, Kiesling shared coaching duties with [[Greasy Neale]] and [[Phil Handler]],{{cite web|url= http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/member.jsp?player_id=114|title= Walt Kiesling|access-date= May 5, 2008|publisher= ProFootballhof.com|archive-date= May 5, 2008|archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20080505054645/http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/member.jsp?player_id=114|url-status= live}} who have not been included within this list. Since the beginning of the Super Bowl era in 1970, the Steelers have had only four head coaches, the fewest of any NFL franchise. Struggling with a losing record for much of the franchise's early years, the team's first season with more wins than losses was coached by [[Jock Sutherland]] in [[1942 Pittsburgh Steelers season|1942]]. In [[1947 Pittsburgh Steelers season|1947]], under Sutherland, the Steelers played their first playoff game against the [[Philadelphia Eagles]]. Ten of the 17 head coaches spent their entire professional coaching careers with the franchise, including Kiesling, [[Johnny Blood]], and [[Chuck Noll]], who have also been voted into the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]].{{cite web|url=http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/teams.html |title=Hall of Famers by Franchise |access-date=April 27, 2008 |publisher=ProFootballHOF.com |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080412001112/http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/teams.html |archive-date=April 12, 2008 |url-status=dead }} One of only four men to coach the same team for 23 years, Noll retired in [[1991 Pittsburgh Steelers season|1991]]. [[Bill Cowher]], who was Noll's replacement, coached the Steelers to their fifth Super Bowl victory in [[Super Bowl XL]] (2005) and was voted into the Hall of Fame in 2020. The Steelers' sixth Super Bowl win came in [[Super Bowl XLIII]] (2008) under head coach [[Mike Tomlin]], who was hired to replace the retiring Cowher in 2007.{{cite press release|title=Steelers Name Mike Tomlin the 16th Head Coach in Team History. |url=http://news.steelers.com/article/73526/|publisher=Pittsburgh Steelers|date=January 23, 2007|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070125160843/http://news.steelers.com/article/73526/|archive-date=January 25, 2007 |access-date=July 14, 2016}}{{cite news|first= Gerry|last= Dulac|title= Tomlin, 34, is NFL's rising coaching star|url= http://www.post-gazette.com/pg/07018/754788-66.stm|newspaper= [[Pittsburgh Post-Gazette]]|date= January 18, 2007|access-date= April 27, 2008|archive-date= May 1, 2007|archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20070501125446/http://www.post-gazette.com/pg/07018/754788-66.stm|url-status= live}} ==Key== {| class="wikitable" style="font-size:90%;" |- |'''#''' |Number of coachesA running total of the number of coaches of the Steelers. New England Patriots:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[File:Mike Vrabel 2023.png|thumb|235x235px|[[Mike Vrabel]], the current head coach of the Patriots, also played for the team as a [[linebacker]] from 2001 to 2008.]] The [[New England Patriots]] are a professional [[American football]] team based in [[Foxborough, Massachusetts]]. They are a member of the [[AFC East|East Division]] of the [[American Football Conference]] (AFC) in the [[National Football League]] (NFL). The team began as the Boston Patriots in the [[American Football League]], a league which [[AFL-NFL merger|merged]] with the [[National Football League]] before the [[1970 NFL season|1970 season]].{{cite web |url=http://www.profootballhof.com/history/team.jsp?franchise_id=19 |title=Boston Patriots |access-date=May 13, 2008 |publisher=Pro Football Hall of Fame |archive-date=June 4, 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080604135922/http://www.profootballhof.com/history/team.jsp?FRANCHISE_ID=19 |url-status=live }} There have been 16 head coaches for the Patriots franchise. [[Lou Saban]] became the first coach of the Patriots in 1960, although he was fired partway through their second season.{{cite web |url=http://www.profootballhof.com/history/team.jsp?FRANCHISE_ID=4 |title=Buffalo Bills |access-date=May 13, 2008 |publisher=Pro Football Hall of Fame |archive-date=June 4, 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080604135926/http://www.profootballhof.com/history/team.jsp?FRANCHISE_ID=4 |url-status=live }} The 16th and current head coach is [[Mike Vrabel]], who was hired on January 12, 2025. He is the second coach in the club's history to have played for the team (2001–2008){{Cite web |last=Goss |first=Nick |date=2025-01-12 |title=Patriots hire Mike Vrabel as next head coach, completing brief search |url=https://www.nbcsportsboston.com/nfl/new-england-patriots/mike-vrabel-hired-head-coach-jerod-mayo/679964/ |access-date=2025-01-12 |website=NBC Sports Boston |language=en-US}}. Longtime head coach [[Bill Belichick]] led the team for more regular season games (387), post-season games (41) and complete seasons (24) than any other head coach. His 266 wins with the Patriots are far and away the most in franchise history, more than five times those of runner-up [[Mike Holovak]]. Belichick also led the team to nine of their eleven [[Super Bowl]] appearances, winning six of them. Holovak, [[Raymond Berry]] and [[Bill Parcells]] all led the Patriots to league championship games, with only one (Holovak) failing to reach the Super Bowl. Five Patriots head coaches, Holovak, [[Chuck Fairbanks]], Berry, Parcells, and Belichick have been named [[National Football League Coach of the Year Award|coach of the year]] by at least one major news organization. Additionally, Raymond Berry and Bill Parcells are members of the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]], with Berry being inducted in 1973, and Parcells in 2013.{{cite web | title = Raymond Berry profile | publisher = Pro Football Hall of Fame | url = http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/member.jsp?PLAYER_ID=25 | access-date = April 24, 2008 | archive-date = May 6, 2008 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20080506052302/http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/member.jsp?player_id=25 | url-status = live }}{{Cite web |title=Bill Parcells {{!}} Pro Football Hall of Fame |url=https://www.profootballhof.com/players/bill-parcells |access-date=January 12, 2024 |website=pfhof |language=en |archive-date=January 11, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240111225229/https://www.profootballhof.com/players/bill-parcells/ |url-status=live }}[[File:Bill Belichick 2019 (cropped).jpg|thumb|[[Bill Belichick]] was the most successful head coach in franchise history, leading the team to six [[Super Bowl]] championships in his 24-year tenure.|178x178px]]Twice in Patriots history there were "interim" head coaches. In 1972, John Mazur resigned with five games left in the season.{{cite news |url=http://archive.boston.com/sports/football/patriots/articles/2005/12/01/for_mazur_the_scars_remain/ |title=For Mazur, the scars remain |access-date=May 14, 2008 |last=Grossfeld |first=Stan |date=December 1, 2005 |work=[[The Boston Globe]] |archive-date=October 24, 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121024115213/http://www.boston.com/sports/football/patriots/articles/2005/12/01/for_mazur_the_scars_remain/?page=1 |url-status=live }} [[Phil Bengtson]] was named as the interim head coach for the rest of the season, during which he only won one game, and he was not made the permanent coach the next year. In 1978, head coach Fairbanks secretly made a deal to leave the team to coach the [[Colorado Buffaloes football|University of Colorado Buffaloes]] while he was still coaching the Patriots. Team owner [[Billy Sullivan (American football)|Billy Sullivan]] suspended Fairbanks for the final game of the regular season, stating "You cannot serve two masters," and [[Ron Erhardt]] and [[Hank Bullough]] took co-head coaching responsibilities for that game.{{cite news |url=http://www.boston.com/sports/football/patriots/superbowl/globe_stories/020502/butt_of_jokes_to_last_laugh+.shtml |title=Butt of jokes to last laugh |access-date=April 26, 2008 |last=Borges |first=Ron |work=[[The Boston Globe]] |archive-date=July 24, 2004 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20040724051437/http://www.boston.com/sports/football/patriots/superbowl/globe_stories/020502/butt_of_jokes_to_last_laugh+.shtml |url-status=dead }} Fairbanks was reinstated when the team qualified for the playoffs, and he lost the first playoff game, his last for the Patriots. He holds NFL record for wins.]] [[File:Mike McDaniel 2023.png|thumb|upright|[[Mike McDaniel]], hired in 2022, was the team's head coach from 2022 to 2025.]] The [[Miami Dolphins]] are a professional [[American football]] franchise based in [[Miami Gardens, Florida|Miami Gardens]], [[Florida]]. They are members of the [[AFC East|East Division]] of the [[American Football Conference]] (AFC) in the [[National Football League]] (NFL). The Dolphins began play in 1966 as an expansion team in the [[American Football League]] (AFL), and joined the NFL as part of the [[AFL–NFL merger]]. The team has played their home games at [[Hard Rock Stadium]], originally known as Joe Robbie Stadium, Pro Player Stadium, Dolphins Stadium, Dolphin Stadium, Landshark Stadium, and Sun Life Stadium, since 1987.{{cite web|url=http://www.dolphinstadium.com/content/history.aspx|title=History|publisher=Dolphin Stadium|access-date=December 6, 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20061024112808/http://www.dolphinstadium.com/content/history.aspx|archive-date=October 24, 2006|url-status=dead}} The Dolphins are currently owned by [[Stephen M. Ross]].{{cite web|url=http://www.miamidolphins.com/newsite/contacts/administration/administration.asp|title=Contacts - Administration|publisher=NFL Enterprises LLC|work=[[Miami Dolphins]]|access-date=December 6, 2008|archive-date=January 21, 2009|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090121133638/http://miamidolphins.com/newsite/contacts/administration/administration.asp|url-status=dead}} There have been fifteen [[head coach]]es for the Dolphins franchise. The team's first head coach was [[George Wilson (football coach)|George Wilson]], who coached for four complete seasons. [[Don Shula]], who coached the Dolphins for 26 consecutive seasons, is the franchise's all-time leader for the most regular-season games coached (392), the most regular-season game wins (257), the most playoff games coached (31), and the most playoff-game wins (17). Shula is also the only Dolphins head coach to win a [[Super Bowl]] with the team, winning two. He was named [[List of NFL Coach of the Year awards|NFL Coach of the Year]] by the [[Associated Press]] (AP), ''[[The Sporting News]]'' (TSN), ''[[Pro Football Weekly]]'' (PFW), and [[United Press International]] (UPI) during his tenure with the Dolphins. The current head coach of the Dolphins, [[Jeff Hafley]], was hired on January 19, 2026.{{Cite web|url=https://www.miamidolphins.com/news/miami-dolphins-agree-to-terms-with-jeff-hafley-as-head-coach|title=Miami Dolphins agree to terms with Jeff Hafley as head coach|publisher=[[Miami Dolphins]]|date=January 19, 2026|accessdate=January 19, 2026}} ==Key== {| class="wikitable" |- |'''#''' |Number of coachesA running total of the number of coaches of the Dolphins. Buffalo Bills:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[Image:RalphWilsonStadiumsign.jpg|thumb|200px|right|The entrance sign to the home of the Bills, [[Ralph Wilson Stadium|Highmark Stadium]] (formerly New Era Field and Ralph Wilson Stadium)]] The [[Buffalo Bills]] are a professional [[American football]] team based in the [[Buffalo, New York]] [[metropolitan area]]. They are members of the [[AFC East|Eastern Division]] of the [[American Football Conference]] (AFC) in the [[National Football League]] (NFL). The Bills franchise was formed in 1960 as a charter member of the [[American Football League]] (AFL), before joining the NFL as part of the [[AFL-NFL merger]] of 1970.{{cite web|url=http://www.nfl.com/history/chronology/1961-1970|title=NFL History by Decade: 1961–1970|work=[[NFL.com]]|access-date=June 21, 2008|archive-date=February 5, 2007|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070205052436/http://www.nfl.com/history/chronology/1961-1970|url-status=live}} There have been 21 head coaches for the Bills franchise. [[Buster Ramsey]] became the first head coach of the Buffalo Bills in 1960, serving for two seasons before being fired by Bills owner [[Ralph Wilson]] after the 1961 season.{{cite web|title=The Month of January In Bills History |publisher=Buffalo Bills |url=http://www.buffalobills.com/history/January.jsp |access-date=June 20, 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080113185105/http://www.buffalobills.com/history/January.jsp |archive-date=January 13, 2008 |url-status=dead }} In terms of tenure, [[Marv Levy]] has coached more games (182) and seasons (12) than any other coach in franchise history. He coached the Bills to four straight [[AFC Championship Game|AFC Championships]] from 1990 to 1993, but failed to lead the team to a victory in the [[Super Bowl]]. One of Levy's predecessors, [[Lou Saban]], who coached the team on two occasions, led the team to the victories in the [[American Football League playoffs|AFL championship in 1964 and 1965]]. Three Bills coaches—Saban, Levy and [[Chuck Knox]]—have been named [[National Football League Coach of the Year Award|coach of the year]] by at least one major news organization.{{cite web|url=http://www.hickoksports.com/history/nflcoy.shtml |title=NFL Coach of the Year Award |last=Hickok |first=Ralph |date=April 17, 2008 |access-date=May 29, 2008 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20121205094433/http://www.hickoksports.com/history/nflcoy.shtml |archive-date=December 5, 2012 }} Levy and [[Jim Ringo]] are the only Bills coaches to have been inducted into the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]]. There have been six "interim" head coaches in Bills history. First, in 1968, head coach [[Joe Collier]] was fired two games into the season and replaced by Bills personnel director [[Harvey Johnson (coach)|Harvey Johnson]].{{cite web|title=The Month of September In Bills History |publisher=Buffalo Bills |url=http://www.buffalobills.com/history/September.jsp |access-date=June 20, 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080607114318/http://www.buffalobills.com/history/September.jsp |archive-date=June 7, 2008 |url-status=dead }} Johnson did not serve as head coach the following season. Then, five games into the 1976 season, Saban unexpectedly resigned as head coach. He was replaced by Ringo, the team's offensive line coach.{{cite web|title=The Month of October In Bills History |publisher=Buffalo Bills |url=http://www.buffalobills.com/history/October.jsp |access-date=June 20, 2008 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071219175559/http://www.buffalobills.com/history/October.jsp |archive-date=December 19, 2007 }} Ringo returned to coach the team again in the 1977 season. In October 1985, [[Kay Stephenson]] was fired and replaced by assistant head coach [[Hank Bullough]]. Just over a year later, Bullough was himself fired and replaced by Marv Levy, who had previously served as coach of the Kansas City Chiefs.{{cite web|title=The Month of November In Bills History |publisher=Buffalo Bills |url=http://www.buffalobills.com/history/November.jsp |access-date=June 20, 2008 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071218101915/http://www.buffalobills.com/history/November.jsp |archive-date=December 18, 2007 }} Levy would then serve as Bills head coach for the next 12 seasons. In 2016, [[Anthony Lynn]] replaced Rex Ryan for the final game of the season. Following Levy's retirement, the Bills experienced limited success under a series of successive head coaches. [[Wade Phillips]], the Bills' defensive coordinator for the last three years under Levy, took over head coaching duties for the 1998 season. Phillips served as head coach for three seasons, making the playoffs in his first two (He was the last coach to lead the Bills to the playoffs until [[Sean McDermott]] became coach in 2017).{{cite web | title=Dallas Cowboys Head Coach | work=NFL.com | url=http://www.nfl.com/teams/coaches?coaType=head&team=DAL | access-date=November 18, 2009 | archive-date=December 1, 2009 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20091201012852/http://www.nfl.com/teams/coaches?coaType=head&team=DAL | url-status=live }} After Phillips' departure following the 2000 season, [[Gregg Williams]] was named head coach.{{cite web | title=Buffalo Bills History | publisher=CBS Interactive | url=http://www.cbssports.com/nfl/teams/history/BUF | access-date=November 18, 2009 | archive-date=August 18, 2011 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110818190413/http://www.cbssports.com/nfl/teams/history/BUF | url-status=live }} Williams served as coach for three seasons. At the end of the 2003 season, Williams' contract was not renewed.{{cite news | title=Fewell picked to replace Jauron as Bills coach | publisher=Associated Press | url=https://www.google.com/hostednews/ap/article/ALeqM5j0dS7CbAH7Y4uxHfie2DYOT-9G5QD9C1JN2O0 | access-date=November 18, 2009}}{{dead link|date=June 2024|bot=medic}}{{cbignore|bot=medic}} [[Mike Mularkey]] was named as the new head coach for the 2004 season, leading the Bills to their first winning season since 1999. The Bills experienced less success under Mularkey during 2005, and Mularkey resigned as head coach at the completion of the 2005 season. The Bills then named [[Dick Jauron]] as their head coach for the 2006 season. Jauron was the first coach since Phillips' dismissal with prior head coaching experience, having previously served as head coach of the [[Chicago Bears]] and interim head coach of the [[Detroit Lions]]. Jauron coached the Bills to three consecutive 7–9 seasons before being fired on November 17, 2009, nine games into the 2009 season. Defensive Coordinator Perry Fewell was named as the interim head coach, going 3–4 to finish out the season before all of the Bills coaching staff was fired on January 4, 2010. On January 19, 2010, the Bills named [[Chan Gailey]] as their next head coach; Gailey was fired on December 31, 2012.{{cite coach saw the team qualify for the playoffs eight times. Despite the success, McDermott was fired on January 19, 2026, after nine seasons with the team.{{Cite web |title=Buffalo Bills announce Sean McDermott has been relieved of his duties as head coach |url=https://www.buffalobills.com/news/buffalo-bills-announce-sean-mcdermott-has-been-relieved-of-his-duties-as-head-coach |access-date=January 19, 2026 |website=www.buffalobills.com |language=en-US}} The Bills then named [[Joe Brady (American football coach)|Joe Brady]], the offensive coordinator for the past three seasons under McDermott, as their next head coach, on January 27, 2026.{{cite web |last1=Getzenberg |first1=Alaina |title=Bills promote OC Joe Brady to head coach on 5-year deal |url=https://www.espn.com/nfl/story/_/id/47744752/sources-bills-promoting-oc-brady-head-coach-5-year-deal |website=ESPN.com |publisher=ESPN |access-date=January 27, 2026 |date=January 27, 2026}} ==Key== {| class="wikitable" |- |'''#''' |Number of coaches {{ref label|Note1|A|A}} |- |'''GC''' |Games coached |- |'''W''' |Wins |- |'''L''' |Losses |- |'''T''' |Ties |- |'''Win%''' |[[Winning percentage]] {{ref label|Note1|B|B}} |- |style="background-color:#FFE6BD"| {{0}}{{0}}† |Elected into the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] as a coach |- |style="background-color:#E4D00A"|{{0}}{{0}}‡ |Elected into the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] as a player |- |style="background-color:#ddffdd"| {{0}}{{0}}* |Spent entire AFL/NFL head coaching career with the Bills |- |style="background-color:#FFFACD"| {{0}}{{0}}** |Two coaching tenures for Lou Saban |- |style="background-color:#FBCEB1"| {{0}}{{0}}^ |Two coaching tenures for Harvey Johnson |} ==Coaches== {| class="wikitable sortable" style="text-align:center" ! Houston Texans:
{{Short description|ALL Houston Texans Head Coaches}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[Image:Gary Kubiak.jpg|thumb|250px|Gary Kubiak led the Texans to their 1st division championship and 1st playoff win.]] There have been six [[head coaches]] of the [[Houston Texans]], a professional [[American football]] team based in [[Houston, Texas|Houston]], [[Texas]], United States. The Texans play in the [[AFC South|South Division]] of the [[American Football Conference]] (AFC) in the [[National Football League]] (NFL). In [[2002 Houston Texans season|2002]], the Texans entered the NFL as an [[expansion team]], with [[Dom Capers]] (2002–05) as the team's first head coach. Capers was selected, in part, due to his success in having led the expansion [[Carolina Panthers]] to the playoffs in just their [[1996 Carolina Panthers season|second season]] in the league. After three straight seasons of consistent improvement, the Texans took a major step backward in [[2005 Houston Texans season|2005]] with a 2–14 finish. At the end of the season, Capers was fired and replaced with [[Gary Kubiak]] (2006–2013), then the [[offensive coordinator]] for the [[Denver Broncos]]. Kubiak, a Houston native and alumnus of nearby [[Texas A&M University|Texas A&M]], would eventually become the longest-tenured head coach in franchise history, coaching 125 games and posting a 61–64 record. Kubiak earned several distinctions as head coach, having led the Texans to their first non-losing season ([[2007 Houston Texans season|2007]]), their first winning season ([[2009 Houston Texans season|2009]]), and their first division title, playoff appearance, and playoff win ([[2011 Houston Texans season|2011]]). In both 2011 and [[2012 Houston Texans season|2012]], the Texans won the AFC South and the first game of the playoffs but lost the divisional game the following week. In [[2013 Houston Texans season|2013]], the Texans started the season 2–0, but then lost their next 11 games. Kubiak was fired mid-season, on December 6, 2013{{cite web|accessdate=December 6, 2013|url=http://msn.foxsports.com/nfl/story/houston-texans-fire-coach-gary-kubiak-120613|title=Houston Texans fire coach Gary Kubiak|work=Fox Sports }} and was replaced on an interim basis by [[defensive coordinator]] [[Wade Phillips]] (2013, 3 games). During the 2013 off-season, [[Bill O'Brien (American football)|Bill O'Brien]] (2014–2020) was hired to be the Texans' next head coach.{{Cite web|url=https://www.usatoday.com/story/sports/nfl/texans/2013/12/31/houston-texans-agree-bill-obrien-head-coach-penn-state/4273287/|title=Texans reach agreement with Bill O'Brien|website=[[USA Today]]}} O'Brien had previously served as head coach at [[Penn State Football|Penn State]] and as offensive coordinator under [[Bill Belichick]] with the [[New England Patriots]]. In [[2020 Houston Texans season|the 2020 season]], the Texans started 0-4, and O'Brien was fired and replaced by interim head coach [[Romeo Crennel]] during the regular season on October 5, 2020.{{cite web|accessdate=October 5, 2020|url=http://www.houstontexans.com/news/romeo-crennel-brings-wealth-of-experience-as-head-coach|title=Romeo Crennel brings wealth of experience as head coach}} During the 2020 offseason, [[David Culley]] was hired as the Texans' next head coach.{{Cite web|url=https://www.houstontexans.com/news/houston-texans-hire-david-culley-as-head-coach|title=Houston Texans Hire David Culley as Head Coach|website=www.houstontexans.com|language=en-US|access-date=January 29, 2021|date=January 29, 2021|first=Houston Texans|last=Public Relations}} Culley had previously served as an assistant head coach and wide receivers coach with multiple NFL franchises, most recently the [[Baltimore Ravens]]. However, he was fired after just one season, with the team posting a 4–13 record during his brief tenure.{{Cite web|publisher=[[Houston Texans]]|url=https://www.houstontexans.com/news/press-release-statements-from-the-houston-texans|title=Statements from the Houston Texans |date=January 13, 2022|access-date=January 13, 2022}}{{cite web|url=https://www.nfl.com/news/houston-texans-david-culley-fire-head-coach-after-one-season|title=Houston Texans fire head coach David Culley after one season|website=[[NFL.com]]|date=January 13, 2022|access-date=January 13, 2022}} During the 2021 offseason, [[Lovie Smith]] was hired as the Texans' next head coach.{{Cite web|url=https://www.houstontexans.com/news/houston-texans-hire-lovie-smith-as-head-coach|title= Houston Texans Hire Lovie Smith as Head Coach|website=www.houstontexans.com|language=en-US|access-date=February 7, 2022|date=February 7, 2022|first=Houston Texans|last=Public Relations}} Smith had previously served as associate head coach and defensive coordinator during the [[2021 Houston Texans season|2021 season]]. Smith was previously the head coach of the [[Chicago Bears]] and the [[Tampa Bay Buccaneers]], as well as the [[Illinois Fighting Illini football|Illinois Fighting Illini]]. Smith, however, was fired after posting a 3–13–1 record, becoming the second-straight Texans head coach to be fired after one season.{{cite web |url=https://www.houstontexans.com/news/statement-from-texans-chair-and-ceo-cal-mcnair-and-general-manager-nick-caserio- |title=Statement from Texans Chair and CEO Cal McNair and General Manager Nick Caserio on Lovie Smith |publisher=Houston Texans |date=January 8, 2023 |access-date=January 9, 2023}} During the 2022 offseason, [[DeMeco Ryans]] was hired as the Texans' next head coach;{{Cite web|url=https://www.houstontexans.com/news/houston-texans-hire-demeco-ryans-as-head-coach|title=Houston Texans hire DeMeco Ryans as Head Coach|website=www.houstontexans.com|language=en-US|access-date=January 31, 2023|date=January 31, 2023|first=Houston Texans|last=Public Relations}} Ryans served as defensive coordinator with the [[San Francisco 49ers]]. New York Jets:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=May 2021}} There have been 22 head coaches in the history of the [[New York Jets]] football franchise. The team began as the New York Titans in the [[American Football League]] in 1960, but was renamed the New York Jets three years later.{{cite web |url=http://www.profootballhof.com/history/team.jsp?FRANCHISE_ID=22 |title=New York Jets |access-date=2008-05-25 |publisher=Pro Football Hall of Fame |archive-date=June 2, 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080602123250/http://www.profootballhof.com/history/team.jsp?FRANCHISE_ID=22 |url-status=live }} The Jets remained in the American Football League until the [[AFL-NFL merger|merger]] with the [[National Football League]] prior to the [[1970 NFL season|1970 season]]. The current head coach of the Jets is [[Aaron Glenn]], who was hired on January 22, 2025.{{Cite web |title=Jets hiring former player, Lions defensive coordinator Aaron Glenn as new head coach |url=https://www.nfl.com/news/jets-hire-aaron-glenn-new-head-coach-lions-defensive-coordinator |access-date=2025-01-22 |website=NFL.com |language=en-US}} [[Sammy Baugh]] became the first head coach of the New York Titans in 1960, serving for two seasons before team owner [[Harry Wismer]] replaced him with [[Bulldog Turner|Clyde "Bulldog" Turner]].{{cite web |url=http://www.newyorkjets.com/team/history/1962.html |title=Year In Review: 1962 |access-date=2008-05-25 |publisher=New York Jets |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100804014816/http://www.newyorkjets.com/team/history/1962.html |archive-date=2010-08-04 }} In terms of tenure, [[Weeb Ewbank]] has coached more games (158) and more complete seasons (11) than any other head coach in franchise history. He led the Jets to the [[AFL playoffs#1968 Championship|AFL championship in 1968]] and the AFL–NFL championship in [[Super Bowl III]]. [[Walt Michaels]] led the team to the AFC championship game in 1982; he was also honored as the [[Pro Football Weekly NFL Coach of the Year]] and [[UPI NFL Coach of the Year|UPI AFC Coach of the Year]] in 1978. Coaches Baugh, Turner and Ewbank are all members of the Pro Football Hall of Fame; Baugh and Turner were inducted as players, while Ewbank was inducted as a coach/administrator. Three times in Jets history, the team has had an "interim" head coach. In 1975, [[Charley Winner]] was fired as head coach after leading the Jets to a 2–7 record.{{cite web |url=http://www.newyorkjets.com/team/history/1975.html |title=Year In Review: 1975 |access-date=2008-05-25 |publisher=New York Jets |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100803220900/http://www.newyorkjets.com/team/history/1975.html |archive-date=2010-08-03 }} The team offensive coordinator [[Ken Shipp]] was named the interim coach for the remainder of the season, during which he won only one of five games. Shipp was succeeded by Lou Holtz for the 1976 season.{{cite web |url=http://www.newyorkjets.com/team/history/1976.html |title=Year In Review: 1976 |access-date=2008-05-25 |publisher=New York Jets |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100804014837/http://www.newyorkjets.com/team/history/1976.html |archive-date=2010-08-04 }} Holtz resigned as Jets head coach with one game left in the 1976 season; [[Mike Holovak]] was named interim coach for the season finale against the [[Cincinnati Bengals]]. In 2024, [[Robert Saleh]] was fired five games into the season, upon which defensive coordinator [[Jeff Ulbrich]] took over as interim head coach.{{cite web |last1=Cimini |first1=Rich |date=October 8, 2024|url=https://www.espn.com/nfl/story/_/id/41679781/jets-fire-coach-robert-saleh-2-3-start-sources-say |title=Jets fire Robert Saleh after 2-3 start, tab Jeff Ulbrich interim|access-date=2024-12-13 |publisher=ESPN }} [[Bill Belichick]] was twice named head coach of the Jets but never coached a single game or practice in that capacity. In 1997, he was named head coach for six days before the deal to allow [[Bill Parcells]] to leave the [[New England Patriots]] for the Jets was brokered, and Belichick became defensive coordinator;{{cite web |title=Parcells to Coach Jets in 1997 |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1997-02-11-sp-27483-story.html |website=Los Angeles Times |access-date=May 14, 2021 |date=February 10, 1997 |archive-date=January 26, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210126094718/https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1997-02-11-sp-27483-story.html |url-status=live }} then, when Parcells stepped down after the 1999 season Belichick was named to replace him, but resigned the next day.{{cite news |last1=Shapiro |first1=Leonard |title=Belichick Suddenly Quits Jets After 1 Day |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/wp-srv/WPcap/2000-01/05/026r-010500-idx.html |newspaper=The Washington Post |access-date=May 14, 2021 |date=January 5, 2000 |archive-date=February 18, 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200218120040/https://www.washingtonpost.com/wp-srv/WPcap/2000-01/05/026r-010500-idx.html |url-status=live }} [[Herman Edwards]] is the only Jets head coach to lead the team to the playoffs more than twice; [[Rex Ryan]] is the only one with more than two postseason wins. [[Todd Bowles]] and [[Robert Saleh]] are the only ones to coach the Jets for more than two seasons without making the playoffs. Two Jets head coaches have played for the Jets; [[Walt Michaels]], who as a defensive assistant coach had to suit up and play in the 1963 season opener due to injuries, and [[Aaron Glenn]], a 1994 first-round draft pick who played cornerback for the team from 1994 to 2001. ==Key== {| class="wikitable" |- |'''#''' |Number of coachesA running total of the number of Jets head coaches. Baltimore Ravens:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[Image:Mandtbankstade.png|thumb|300px|Satellite picture of the Ravens' home, [[M&T Bank Stadium]]]] [[File:John Harbaugh 2012.jpg|upright|thumb|[[John Harbaugh]] is the longest-serving head coach of the Ravens, coaching the franchise from [[2008 NFL season|2008]] to [[2025 NFL season|2025]]. He is the most successful head coach in franchise history.]] The [[Baltimore Ravens]] are an [[American football]] team based in [[Baltimore|Baltimore, Maryland]]. They are currently members of the North division of the [[American Football Conference]] (AFC) in the [[National Football League]] (NFL). In 1996, the Ravens joined the [[American Football Conference]] (AFC) of the [[National Football League]] (NFL) after former [[Cleveland Browns]] owner [[Art Modell]] decided to relocate the team to Baltimore. However, as part of an agreement between Modell, the NFL and the city of Cleveland, Modell left the Browns' name, colors and history in Cleveland. However, the team's players and staff were permitted to move to Baltimore with the franchise. For this reason, the Ravens are considered an [[expansion team]]. The Browns name was later reintroduced in 1999.{{cite news|url=https://query.nytimes.com/gst/fullpage.html?res=9A07E1DC1539F933A25751C0A960958260|title=N.F.L. Gives Modell a Ticket to Baltimore|last=Sandomir|first=Richard|date=February 10, 1996|work=[[The New York Times]]|access-date=May 25, 2008}} Modell had planned to bring [[Bill Belichick]] to Baltimore as head coach, but Belichick was fired February 14, 1996. Instead, Modell hired [[Ted Marchibroda]] on February 15 as the first head coach of the Ravens franchise in 1996. Marchibroda had previously coached the [[Baltimore Colts]] in the 1970s and the [[Indianapolis Colts]] in the 1990s. After three seasons, his contract was not renewed. [[Brian Billick]] succeeded him after accepting a six-year coaching contract from Modell. Billick went on to lead the Ravens to four playoff appearances in his nine years as coach. In 2000, he led the Ravens to a 34–7 victory in [[Super Bowl XXXV]] against the [[New York Giants]].{{cite web|url=http://www.baltimoreravens.com/News/History/Baltimore_Football_History.aspx|title=Baltimore Football History|publisher=Baltimore Ravens|access-date=May 25, 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100211225719/http://www.baltimoreravens.com/News/History/Baltimore_Football_History.aspx|archive-date=February 11, 2010|url-status=dead}} On December 31, 2007, Billick was fired by Ravens owner [[Steve Bisciotti]] after leading the Ravens to a 5–11 record in the [[2007 NFL season|2007 season]]. Less than three weeks later, the Ravens hired [[John Harbaugh]] as the franchise's third head coach.{{cite news|url=http://nbcsports.msnbc.com/id/22734839/|title=Ravens settle for Harbaugh as their new coach|date=January 18, 2008|work=[[Associated Press]]|access-date=May 25, 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080514120836/http://nbcsports.msnbc.com/id/22734839/|archive-date=May 14, 2008|url-status=dead}} Harbaugh reached the playoffs in each of his first five seasons as Ravens head coach, bringing success to the franchise, including a victory in [[Super Bowl XLVII]], in which the Ravens beat the [[San Francisco 49ers]], led by John's brother [[Jim Harbaugh]], by a score of 34–31. ==Coaches== {| class="wikitable" |- |'''#''' |Number of coachesA running total of the number of coaches of the Ravens. Cincinnati Bengals:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[File:Cincinnati-paul-brown-stadium.jpg|thumb|The Bengals have played in [[Paul Brown Stadium]] since 2000.]] This is a '''complete list of Cincinnati Bengals head coaches'''. There have been ten head coaches for the [[Cincinnati Bengals]] of the [[National Football League]] (NFL). A professional [[American football]] team based in [[Cincinnati, Ohio]], the Bengals are a member of the [[AFC North|North Division]] of the [[American Football Conference]] (AFC). The Bengals franchise was founded in 1968 as a member of the Western Division of the [[American Football League]] (AFL),{{cite web|title=History – Cincinnati Bengals|url=http://www.bengals.com/team/history.html|work=[[Cincinnati Bengals]]|access-date=June 15, 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140106223648/http://www.bengals.com/team/history.html|archive-date=January 6, 2014|url-status=dead}} before merging with the NFL in [[AFL-NFL merger|1970]]. The current head coach is [[Zac Taylor]], who was hired after the 2018 season. Taylor replaced [[Marvin Lewis]], who remains the Bengals all-time leader in seasons coached, games coached, wins, and playoff games coached. Three coaches have won a conference championship with the team: [[Forrest Gregg]] in 1981, [[Sam Wyche]] in 1988 and [[Zac Taylor|Taylor]] in 2021.{{cite web|title=Cincinnati Bengals Championship History|url=http://www.nflteamhistory.com/nfl_teams/cincinnati_bengals/championship_history.html|work=NFL Team History.com|access-date=June 15, 2011}} Gregg leads all coaches in winning percentage with .561.{{cite web|title=Forrest Gregg Records, Statistics, and Category Ranks|url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/GregFo0.htm|work=[[Pro-Football-Reference.com]]|publisher=Sports Reference|access-date=June 15, 2011}} [[Dick LeBeau]] had the lowest winning percentage, with .267.{{cite web|title=Dick LeBeau Records, Statistics, and Category Ranks|url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/LeBeDi0.htm|work=[[Pro-Football-Reference.com]]|publisher=Sports Reference|access-date=June 15, 2011}} Of the ten Bengals head coaches, three have been elected to the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]]: [[Paul Brown]], [[Forrest Gregg]], and [[Dick LeBeau]] (although only Brown was inducted as a coach, the other two were inducted as players).{{cite web|title=Hall of Famers by Franchise|url=http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/teams.aspx|publisher=[[Pro Football Hall of Fame]]|access-date=June 15, 2011}} Two former players have been head coach for the Bengals: [[Sam Wyche]] and [[Bruce Coslet]]. ==Key== {| class="wikitable" |- |'''#''' |Number of coachesA running total of the number of coaches of the Bengals.
